review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated push‑notification workflow in Twilio Segment that triggers a personalized SMS/WhatsApp alert at least 24 hours before any schedule change, including the new departure time, gate number, and alternative travel options. | - Integrate a real‑time disruption dashboard (e.g., Power BI integrated with FlightAware API) into the airline’s mobile app so passengers see instant updates and receive a one‑click rebooking option via the Amadeus NDC API. | - Implement a rule‑based compensation engine in Salesforce Service Cloud that automatically issues a $50 travel voucher or lounge access to any passenger whose flight is rescheduled by more than 6 hours, with the voucher generated instantly through the built‑in coupon module. | - Launch a self‑service “Reschedule Assistant” chatbot on Microsoft Teams that uses Azure Cognitive Language Understanding to surface the top three rebooking options and captures satisfaction scores after each interaction for continuous improvement.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Twilio Segment, Power BI, Amadeus NDC, Salesforce, Azure CLU) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 because each recommendation requires significant integration work (e.g., Power BI with FlightAware, Amadeus NDC API, Salesforce coupon engine), cross-team coordination, and likely new development—making them high-effort, expensive, and not deployable 'today' with existing systems. This violates the strict feasibility threshold for a 4 or 5.

Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Customer service representatives gave contradictory information and seemed incompetent.
fixes: Deploy a searchable knowledge‑base in Zendesk Guide and require all agents to complete a mandatory “Article Confidence” quiz (≥90 % pass) before handling any topic, with weekly compliance reports reviewed by the team lead. | - Integrate PlayVox’s real‑time call‑monitoring dashboard into the contact‑center workflow so supervisors flag calls flagged “Inconsistent Info” and schedule a 15‑minute one‑on‑one coaching session within 24 hours. | - Schedule bi‑weekly 45‑minute role‑play drills using the Gong.io conversation analytics platform where agents record mock interactions on high‑volume issues, receive AI‑generated feedback scores, and set a target improvement score of +10 % month‑over‑month. | - Implement a Power Automate workflow that cross‑checks agent responses against the master knowledge‑base and automatically creates a ticket in ServiceNow if the response deviates more than 5 % from the approved script, ensuring immediate corrective action.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to inconsistent agent responses. Each is actionable with clear steps and names specific tools (Zendesk Guide, PlayVox, Gong.io, Power Automate, ServiceNow). Concreteness is excellent. However, feasibility is low (2): the Power Automate workflow requiring cross-system integration with ServiceNow and real-time response deviation detection is not trivial, especially with a 5% threshold; it likely requires custom development, data syncing, and risk of false positives. This is high-effort, not low or trivial.

review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Refund processing took excessively long, with repeated 6‑week delays.
fixes: Deploy an automated refund workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that auto‑creates a case, assigns it to a “Refunds” queue, and enforces a 48‑hour SLA with escalation alerts if the status remains “Pending.” | - Integrate Stripe’s Refund API with your order management system (e.g., Shopify) so refunds are initiated instantly and logged in real time, eliminating manual back‑office steps. | - Implement a daily Power BI dashboard that tracks average refund cycle time, flags any case exceeding 72 hours, and sends Slack notifications to the finance lead for immediate review. | - Establish a dedicated “Refund Specialist” role with a KPI of ≤5 business days per refund; schedule bi‑weekly 15‑minute stand‑up meetings where the specialist presents open cases and the team re‑prioritizes based on the dashboard metrics.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the 6-week refund delay issue with specific, measurable actions. Each includes concrete tools (Salesforce, Stripe, Power BI, Slack) and exact metrics (48-hour SLA, 72-hour threshold, 5-day KPI). The only deduction is in feasibility: while the recommendations are clear and specific, the integration of Stripe’s API with Shopify and the deployment of a dedicated Refund Specialist role with bi-weekly stand-ups require cross-team coordination, new role creation, and ongoing process change—moderate effort, not trivial. Thus, feasibility is 3, not 5.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based virtual queue (e.g., QLess) that issues SMS ticket numbers and displays live estimated wait times on digital signage, reducing on‑site congestion by at least 30% within 45 days. | - Integrate a workforce‑management platform such as Kronos WFM to forecast peak check‑in periods from POS data and automatically schedule additional agents 15 minutes before predicted spikes. | - Install self‑service kiosks (e.g., NCR SelfServ) equipped with QR code scanning for room keys, cutting manual agent interactions by up to 40% during high traffic windows. | - Implement a real‑time performance dashboard in Power BI that tracks average handling time per agent; set a weekly KPI of ≤ 5 minutes per guest and trigger alerts when any agent exceeds the threshold. | - Launch a mobile pre‑check‑in app linked to the property management system (PMS) that allows guests to upload ID and key cards ahead of arrival, eliminating the need for on‑site document verification for 80% of arrivals.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issues of long check-in wait times and inefficient agents. Each is specific, names real tools (QLess, Kronos, NCR, Power BI, PMS), and includes measurable outcomes. The only deduction is for feasibility: while some (like the mobile app and kiosks) are moderate effort due to hardware/software integration and coordination, they are not trivial. The score reflects that implementation is realistic but not 'trivial'—hence a 3, not a 5.

fixes: Deploy handheld POS tablets (e.g., Toast Go) linked to the catering inventory system so cabin crew can instantly place meal requests, track order status, and receive real‑time kitchen confirmations, cutting service time by at least 20 %. | - Integrate a predictive analytics model in Power BI that forecasts peak passenger demand per flight and automatically adjusts staffing levels via an API call to the workforce management platform (e.g., Kronos), ensuring a minimum of one attendant per 15 passengers during high‑traffic periods. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey sent through the airline’s mobile app using Qualtrics, triggering an automated compensation voucher for any rating ≤3 on “service speed” and logging the response to a weekly KPI dashboard for immediate corrective action. | - Install RFID‑enabled beverage carts with barcode scanners that log each item served; the data feeds into a Tableau dashboard where crew performance is measured against a 90 % on‑time delivery target and coaching alerts are generated when thresholds are missed.

Thus, despite strong alignment and specificity, feasibility is severely compromised.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital pre‑flight seat‑condition checklist in the crew’s mobile app (e.g., via AirCrewConnect) that requires photo evidence of each table latch and adhesion test; the system flags any “fail” items for immediate corrective action before boarding. | - Install quick‑release latch kits sourced from supplier X and train mechanics to replace all sticky‑tape fixes within 24 hours, logging installation dates in the existing CMMS (e.g., Fiix). | - Introduce a post‑boarding verification step where cabin crew scan a QR code on each seat, which triggers a real‑time alert in the operations dashboard if the table is not locked properly. | - Create a KPI “Seat Table Integrity Rate” measured weekly; set a target ≥99.5 % and automatically suspend aircraft from service if the rate falls below threshold for two consecutive weeks. | - Launch a micro‑learning module on the LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors) that demonstrates proper table‑installation procedures and requires a 90 % pass quiz before crew members are cleared for duty.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the specific issue of broken, poorly secured seat tables. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete tools (e.g., AirCrewConnect, Fiix, LMS). Concreteness is maximal with named systems and measurable KPIs. However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: requiring immediate replacement of all sticky-tape fixes across fleets, real-time dashboard integration, automated suspension of aircraft, and mandatory micro-learning with quiz pass rates—all of which demand significant coordination, software development, and training rollout, far exceeding trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy digital signage at the immigration hall (e.g., Samsung Smart Signage) linked to the airline’s real‑time “Boarding Pass Collection” feed so passengers see exact locations instantly upon landing. | - Integrate the mobile app with the airport’s Wayfinding API to push a push notification to phones within 5 minutes of arrival, including a QR code that opens a Google Maps page showing the exact collection point. | - Place QR codes on all ticket kiosks and baggage claim carousels that scan to a short video hosted on Vimeo; schedule these videos to play automatically on all airport screens during peak hours. | - Create a Power BI dashboard tracking “Time from Arrival to Boarding Pass Collection” per flight, set a KPI of ≤2 minutes, and trigger an automated Slack alert when any flight exceeds the threshold for immediate crew intervention.
rationale: All recommendations are directly relevant to the issue of unclear boarding pass collection locations. Each is highly actionable with clear steps and specific tools (e.g., Samsung Smart Signage, Wayfinding API, Vimeo, Power BI, Slack). Concreteness is maximal, naming real tools and metrics. However, feasibility is low: deploying digital signage across an immigration hall, integrating with an airport’s Wayfinding API, and hosting videos on Vimeo for automatic playback during peak hours require significant coordination, external vendor access, and infrastructure changes—none of which are trivial or low-cost. This exceeds moderate effort and qualifies as high effort (score 2).

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: In-flight crew served meals while passengers were dozing and expected tables to be set, leading to poor service timing and inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑crew task‑management app (e.g., AirOps) with built‑in timers that automatically notify staff when the “Meal Service” stage begins, preventing premature service while passengers are sleeping. | - Install RFID‑enabled tray racks at each seat row and integrate them with the airline’s inventory system so that the service checklist is displayed on the crew’s handheld device only after all seats have been scanned as “clear.” | - Introduce a pre‑flight passenger‑activity survey in the airline’s mobile app that records sleep duration; feed this data into a Power BI dashboard to generate real‑time alerts when >20% of seats show recent “sleep” flags before meal delivery. | - Conduct a weekly 15‑minute “service synchronization” drill using VR scenario simulations (e.g., Strivr) where crews practice matching table‑setting completion timestamps with the scheduled meal window, tracking performance metrics in a shared Google Sheet.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the service timing issue and explicitly name concrete tools (AirOps, RFID, Power BI, Strivr, Google Sheets). Actionability is strong, though minor interpretation is needed (e.g., 'feed data into Power BI' lacks exact integration steps). Concreteness is excellent with specific tools and metrics. Feasibility is low—RFID rack installation, VR drills, and cross-system integration require high effort, cost, and months of coordination, scoring a 2 despite the strong logic.

review_text: The ground crew were unreasonable and very rude. We were denied boarding as according to them we were 4 minutes late.  The ground crew did a lousy job of offloading our luggage. We had to wait almost half a day in the baggage carousel before filling a missing bag report.
issue: Ground crew were rude and denied boarding for a minor 4‑minute delay
fixes: Deploy a real‑time delay notification app (e.g., FlightAware API integrated with crew tablets) that automatically pushes any delay < 10 minutes to ground staff so they can pre‑announce changes and prevent denial of boarding. | - Institute a “Passenger Courtesy Checklist” in the crew’s SOP, tracked on a mobile form tool such as ServiceNow Mobile, requiring staff to log a brief interaction script and confirmation before closing the boarding gate. | - Set up a weekly 15‑minute micro‑learning session on the LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors) where scenario videos of rude interactions are reviewed and followed by a 3‑question quiz; completion rates must exceed 95 %. | - Implement a passenger experience KPI dashboard in Power BI that records “Boarding Denied Incidents” per month, with a target of ≤0.5 % and automatic alerts to operations managers when thresholds are breached.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause—rude behavior and unjust boarding denial due to minor delays. Each is specific, uses real tools (FlightAware, ServiceNow, SAP SuccessFactors, Power BI), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is penalized at 3 because integrating FlightAware API with crew tablets and rolling out a new KPI dashboard with automated alerts likely requires cross-team coordination, API licensing, and system integration beyond existing tools—moderate effort, not trivial.

review_text: Our first flight was delayed more than one hour, so we lost our connecting flight. The new booking provided was horrible. I was travelling with my four-year-old and the new seat provided is far away, and we were separated. All the family members are separated for the whole journey. Miscommunication between the crew members made us to walk between the terminals for 2 times. Didn't get any help from the crew, even though we requested to provide a seat together.
issue: Flight was delayed over an hour, causing a missed connecting flight
fixes: Deploy a real‑time delay prediction engine (e.g., FlightAware API + Azure Machine Learning) that triggers automatic rebooking alerts in the airline’s CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud) when projected wait time exceeds 45 minutes, ensuring passengers receive alternative connections before departure. | - Integrate Twilio SMS/WhatsApp notifications into the passenger mobile app so any delay exceeding 15 minutes sends an instant message with the new gate, boarding time, and a one‑click “rebook” link to the next available flight. | - Implement an automated compensation workflow in the revenue management system (Sabre AirVision) that issues a $50 travel voucher and a free lounge pass via email within 10 minutes of a confirmed delay, reducing post‑flight complaints by at least 20 %. | - Set up a dedicated “Delay Response Team” in Zendesk with a SLA of ≤ 5 minutes to handle missed‑connection cases, equipped with a pre‑built template and direct access to the crew scheduling portal to secure standby seats on the next flight.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to flight delay remediation, crystal clear in execution, and name specific tools (e.g., FlightAware, Twilio, Sabre AirVision, Zendesk). However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying a real-time prediction engine, integrating multiple APIs, building automated workflows across CRM, revenue systems, and mobile apps, and establishing a dedicated team with cross-system access requires significant engineering, legal, and organizational change—far beyond trivial or low effort.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy an automated disruption‑management workflow in the airline’s OPSControl (or Amadeus Altéa) system that instantly triggers a standby seat on the next available connecting flight when a landing delay exceeds 15 minutes, with the seat offer sent via SMS/WhatsApp within 5 minutes of detection. | - Integrate a real‑time rebooking engine such as Sabre Red Workspace that automatically applies the “first‑flight‑out” rule and assigns passengers to the earliest feasible alternative flight, updating the PNR and notifying them through the airline’s mobile app. | - Launch a dedicated “Disruption Response Team” staffed by two agents per shift who follow a SOP that requires contacting affected travelers within 10 minutes of a delay, logging the interaction in Salesforce Service Cloud, and closing the case only after the passenger receives a confirmed new itinerary. | - Implement a post‑incident analytics dashboard in Power BI that tracks average delay‑to‑rebooking time, missed‑connection rate, and compensation payout, setting a target of ≤ 20 minutes and reviewing weekly to drive continuous improvement.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root issue of missed connections due to landing delays. Each is highly actionable with clear steps and specific tools (e.g., Amadeus Altéa, Sabre Red Workspace, Salesforce Service Cloud, Power BI). Concreteness is maximal with named systems, KPIs, and time targets. Feasibility is capped at 3 because integrating real-time rebooking engines and deploying a dedicated response team requires significant cross-functional coordination and budget, exceeding trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing platform (e.g., Zendesk) with automated SLA alerts that trigger after 24 hours without response, routing any overdue tickets to a “Rapid Response” queue staffed by two dedicated agents during peak hours. | - Integrate a real‑time chatbot on the website and mobile app using Intercom’s Answer Bot, programmed to capture essential details and instantly create tickets for human agents while displaying estimated wait times. | - Implement a workforce‑management solution such as NICE IEX to forecast call‑center volume, schedule additional agents 15 minutes before predicted peaks, and publish daily adherence reports to senior leadership. | - Launch an automated outbound callback feature in the call‑center IVR (using Genesys Cloud) that offers callers a scheduled callback within 5 minutes rather than holding them, reducing average hold time by at least 40 %.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the customer’s issue of unresponsiveness and long hold times. Each is specific, action-oriented, and names real tools (Zendesk, Intercom, NICE IEX, Genesys Cloud). The only deduction is in feasibility: while each tool is real, implementing all four—especially NICE IEX and Genesys Cloud—requires significant budget, integration effort, and cross-functional coordination, making them moderate-to-high effort, not trivial. Thus, feasibility is capped at 3.

After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Lack of dining on demand and average-quality food during the flight.
fixes: Deploy a mobile‑order app (e.g., SITA Cabin) that lets passengers request meals up to 24 hours before departure and receive real‑time kitchen status updates, reducing “on‑demand” wait times by at least 40 %. | - Install IoT temperature sensors linked to the catering ERP (e.g., SAP Meal Management) to monitor meal quality in transit; set automated alerts when temperatures deviate ±2 °C from target, ensuring every dish meets freshness standards. | - Integrate a QR‑code feedback loop on seatback screens that logs passenger taste ratings instantly; feed these scores into a weekly Power BI dashboard to trigger menu‑revision cycles whenever average satisfaction falls below 80 %. | - Contract a regional micro‑cuisine vendor network via a procurement platform such as Coupa, mandating quarterly audits of ingredient sourcing and delivery timeliness with KPIs of ≥95 % on‑time delivery and ≤5 % waste per flight.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to food quality and on-demand dining. Each is actionable with clear steps and concrete tools (e.g., SITA Cabin, SAP, Power BI, Coupa). Concreteness is excellent with specific metrics and systems named. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying a mobile app, integrating IoT sensors with ERP, implementing QR feedback on seatback screens, and contracting a regional vendor network with audits—all require significant capital, cross-functional coordination, and months of execution, making them far from trivial or low-effort.

issue: No touchscreen TV or comparable in‑flight entertainment provided.
fixes: Deploy Panasonic Avionics eX3 seatback IFE (or Viasat Ka‑band Wi‑Fi streaming) on all aircraft within 12 months, scheduling installations during scheduled heavy‑maintenance C‑checks and tracking progress via a centralized SAP PM work order dashboard. | - Integrate FlightAware’s real‑time aircraft positioning API with the airline’s mobile app to push personalized content playlists based on flight duration and time zone, launching the feature in Q4 2023 and measuring adoption through Google Analytics event counts. | - Negotiate a seat‑back HDMI port retrofit contract with Thales/SES, targeting a 15% fleet rollout by December 2024 and monitoring installation completion rates in the existing ServiceNow asset‑management module. | - Launch a quarterly “Entertainment Pulse” survey linked to Medallia, setting a target NPS ≥ 70 for the new IFE experience and automatically routing any score ≤ 6 to a dedicated CX remediation queue for immediate follow‑up. | - Create a cloud‑based content management system (e.g., AWS MediaTailor) to upload licensed movies/series nightly, schedule automatic backups, and track usage metrics to ensure at least 90% of flights offer ≥ 150 minutes of on‑demand programming per passenger segment.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (SAP PM, ServiceNow, Medallia, AWS MediaTailor, etc.) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying IFE systems across a fleet within 12 months, retrofitting HDMI ports, and launching new content systems all require significant capital, vendor contracts, and integration work—none of which are trivial or low-cost. These are not 'existing tools' or 'today' rollouts, violating the strict feasibility threshold.

issue: Seat was average and aircraft was noisy, leading to uncomfortable travel experience.
fixes: Deploy cabin‑wide Active Noise Control (ANC) modules sourced from Siemens Hearing Solutions and integrate them with the aircraft’s existing audio system, targeting ≥15 dB reduction in cabin noise within 6 months. | - Install lightweight, high‑density memory foam seat cushions on all economy rows and replace standard headrests with adjustable “ComfyFit” units; track passenger comfort scores via the Qualtrics post‑flight survey and aim for a minimum 0.8 increase in the “seat comfort” rating over the next quarter. | - Introduce a quarterly “Seat Wear Audit” using the IBM Maximo CMMS to measure recline mechanism wear and upholstery degradation, scheduling replacement parts based on predefined thresholds (e.g., <80 % functionality). | - Launch a real‑time cabin‑environment monitoring dashboard powered by AWS IoT Greengrass sensors that logs temperature, humidity, and vibration levels; set automated alerts when any parameter exceeds preset comfort limits so crew can intervene instantly. | - Conduct a rapid A/B trial of new ergonomic seat prototypes (e.g., ZF Aerospace’s “FlexBack” design) on two flight routes, collecting NPS data for the test group versus the baseline; if the pilot yields a ≥10 point improvement, roll out fleet‑wide within six months.
rationale: While all recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete—specifically naming tools (Siemens, Qualtrics, IBM Maximo, AWS IoT Greengrass, ZF Aerospace)—they are entirely infeasible. Each requires major capital investment, new hardware deployment, fleet-wide integration, or long-term pilots. No existing infrastructure supports these systems, and none are deployable 'today' with zero cost. Feasibility is rated 1 due to financial ruin risk and operational impossibility without multi-year planning.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled boarding passes and integrate them with a real‑time queue‑management dashboard (e.g., Queue-it) so ground staff can see exactly which zones are full and automatically reassign boarding groups on the fly. | - Introduce a “fast‑lane” self‑service bag drop using SITA BagDrop kiosks programmed to capture passenger weight data in advance, reducing manual sorting time by at least 20 % per flight. | - Implement predictive boarding software such as AirlineBoardingAI to generate optimal boarding orders based on seat occupancy and passenger behavior, then push those orders to gate screens and crew tablets via the airline’s OpsControl app. | - Launch a mobile pre‑boarding service (via the airline’s app) that lets passengers select their preferred boarding window and receive automated SMS reminders, thereby smoothing load across multiple gates and cutting overall boarding time by up to 15 %.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Queue-it, SITA BagDrop, AirlineBoardingAI, OpsControl app) and measurable outcomes. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to the high effort and cost of deploying RFID-enabled boarding passes, integrating predictive software, and launching a new mobile pre-boarding service—none of which are trivial or low-effort. These require significant infrastructure, software development, and cross-functional coordination, making them infeasible for immediate rollout.

fixes: Upgrade seat cushion cores to high‑resilience memory foam sourced from FoamPartner, increasing lateral support by at least 15 % and meeting ISO 16840‑2 standards for premium economy seats.** | - **Install motorized recline mechanisms (e.g., Recaro’s “FlexFit” actuators) with a maximum 12° pitch and integrate them via the airline’s existing Cabin Management System to enable crew‑controlled recline during takeoff and landing.** | - **Replace the current 3‑3‑3 cabin layout with a 2‑4‑2 configuration on aircraft ≥150 seats, verified through a CAD‑driven ergonomics simulation using Siemens NX to ensure each row has ≥18″ of aisle clearance and a minimum seat pitch of 32”.** | - **Launch a quarterly “Seat Comfort Survey” in Qualtrics targeting a Net Promoter Score of ≥70; tie any score below this threshold to a corrective‑action ticket in Jira and schedule follow‑up within two weeks.** | - **Add removable lumbar pads manufactured by ErgoPlus, equipped with RFID tags that trigger a maintenance alert when usage count exceeds 500 cycles, ensuring consistent replacement intervals.**

fixes: Deploy a calibrated audio test suite (e.g., Audio Precision APx525) on every seat during final assembly and post‑replacement checks, logging pass/fail results in the existing ERP’s quality module for traceability. | - Implement a “Seat‑Level Speaker Health” dashboard in ServiceNow that tracks left‑channel failure counts per aircraft and automatically generates a work order when failures exceed a predefined threshold (e.g., >2 per month). | - Integrate a step‑by‑step diagnostic flowchart into the crew’s electronic maintenance manual (via the AMOS system) that uses built‑in oscilloscope probes to isolate speaker driver or amplifier issues within 5 minutes. | - Launch a weekly preventive‑maintenance schedule using the IBM Maximo CMMS to replace all left‑channel speakers older than 12 months, with automatic part‑order generation linked to the inventory API. | - Add a QR‑code sticker on each seat that directs passengers to a short troubleshooting video hosted on the airline’s LMS; capture view‑through rates to gauge awareness and reduce repeat calls.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the in-flight audio channel failure issue with specific tools (Audio Precision, ServiceNow, AMOS, IBM Maximo, LMS) and measurable actions. Not impossible, but not trivial either.

Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Cabin crew ignored the passenger’s after‑dinner drink request, showing inattentiveness
fixes: Deploy a tablet‑based service‑request app (e.g., ServiceNow Mobile) integrated with the cabin inventory system so passengers can tap “After‑Dinner Drink” at any time, automatically routing the order to the nearest crew member’s device and logging fulfillment timestamps for real‑time monitoring. | - Install a cabin‑crew performance dashboard in the cockpit (Power BI or Tableau) that pulls data from the request‑app log every flight; set a KPI that ≥95 % of after‑dinner drink requests are logged within 2 minutes, triggering an alert if the threshold is missed. | - Conduct weekly 15‑minute micro‑learning simulations on the VR platform Strivr, where crews practice handling multi‑task scenarios including after‑meal drinks, with performance scores tied to quarterly bonus calculations. | - Introduce a post‑flight QR‑code survey linked to Medallia that asks passengers to rate “Promptness of after‑meal drink delivery”; feed the results into the crew evaluation matrix and tie a monthly incentive to achieving ≥4.5/5 average ratings.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (ServiceNow Mobile, Power BI, Strivr, Medallia) and measurable KPIs. However, feasibility is rated 2 because deploying a tablet-based app, integrating it with cabin inventory, installing a cockpit dashboard, running VR simulations, and launching a post-flight survey system requires significant budget, cross-department coordination, and infrastructure changes—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation. These are not 'today' fixes but multi-month, high-cost initiatives.

issue: No soap was provided in the washroom
fixes: Deploy a digital “Soap Stock Tracker” on tablets at each restroom (e.g., via iAuditor) with daily automatic refill alerts sent to the facilities team when soap levels fall below a preset threshold. | - Install sensor‑activated soap dispensers linked to a cloud‑based analytics platform such as VergeSense; set a KPI that 100 % of dispensers remain operational and trigger maintenance tickets instantly when usage drops >20 % from baseline. | - Add a mandatory “Soap Availability” checkbox to the weekly cleaning checklist in Asana, assigning a compliance scorecard and automatically flagging any location where the box remains unchecked after two days. | - Implement QR‑code feedback stations on bathroom walls that log “no soap” incidents directly into ServiceNow, routing them to a dedicated “Washroom Hygiene” queue with a 24‑hour SLA for resolution.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the specific issue of missing soap in washrooms with precise, measurable actions. Each specifies real tools (iAuditor, VergeSense, Asana, ServiceNow), concrete KPIs, and clear triggers. The only deduction is in feasibility: sensor-activated dispensers and cloud analytics platforms (e.g., VergeSense) require significant upfront investment and integration, making them moderate effort, not trivial. Thus, feasibility is 3, not 5.

fixes: Deploy a mandatory checklist in the POS (e.g., Toast POS) that requires staff to select “Offer disinfectant kit” before finalizing any table; configure the system so the order cannot be completed without this step being ticked. | - Add a QR‑code prompt on each seat‑back card that links to a real‑time inventory API (using Zapier + Google Sheets) showing current stock levels of disinfectant kits; staff must scan the code and confirm availability before offering. | - Install a digital signage dashboard at the front desk that displays a daily “Disinfection Offer Compliance” metric pulled from the POS analytics, with a target ≥ 95 % and automatic alerts when the rate falls below threshold. | - Schedule weekly 15‑minute “Disinfectant Kit Audit” huddles where floor managers review the previous week’s compliance data in a Power BI report, assign corrective actions, and log them in Asana for tracking.

No points deducted for format or clarity.

issue: Crew appeared overworked and demotivated, affecting overall service
fixes: Deploy a workforce‑management platform such as Kronos WFM to forecast peak traffic, generate optimal shift rosters, and enforce a maximum‑hours cap (e.g., ≤45 hrs/week) with automated alerts when schedules exceed limits. | - Institute a weekly 15‑minute “pulse check” huddle where supervisors use a short survey in Qualtrics (3‑question engagement score) to identify burnout triggers; trigger corrective actions if average scores fall below 8/10 for two consecutive weeks. | - Launch a gamified incentive program on Bonusly that awards points for meeting predefined performance metrics (first‑call resolution, upsell rate, attendance), with monthly top‑performer announcements to boost morale and recognize contributions. | - Create a dedicated “Wellness Champion” role within each team who logs staff absenteeism and stress‑leave data in an HRIS like SAP SuccessFactors, then coordinates targeted coaching sessions via the Lessonly microlearning library for employees flagged by the system.
